title: The scorecard ritual: marking, attesting, signing
microFact: In competition you keep your PARTNER's card, not your own. Signing a wrong card has ended major championships.
lesson:
Competition scorekeeping is a ritual with real rules:

• On the first tee, cards are exchanged: you become the MARKER for another player. You record THEIR gross score hole-by-hole (your own too, in a spare row, for cross-checking).
• After each hole, confirm verbally: "6?" — "yes, 6."
• After the round: review, resolve differences, then the marker SIGNS (attests) the card and the player countersigns. Hand it to the committee.
• Signing for a score LOWER than actual = disqualification, even if accidental. Higher than actual = the higher score stands. This is why everyone double-checks — and why Roberto De Vicenzo lost the 1968 Masters ("What a stupid I am").

Casual rounds relax all this — one person often keeps the group's card (or an app; 18Birdies and Hole19 are common, and IGU WHS posting needs your attested gross anyway).

Habit to build now: announce your score walking off every green, honestly, including penalties. "Seven — six and a penalty." Count everything. Reputation compounds.

quiz.question: You accidentally sign your card with a 5 on a hole where you made 6. What happens in stroke play?
quiz.explanation: Signing for lower than actual = DQ. Signing for higher = the higher score stands. Check every hole before signing.
